task.name: Planning supervisor — parallel spec-writing orchestrator
task.objective: Spawn N parallel /tmux:execute workers via tmux-cli to produce implementation-ready specification files. Read a plan/spec file, decompose it into modules, create research-task context files, spawn spec-writing workers, verify spec quality against the S1–S8 pushback catalogue, present specs for interactive user approval, and finalize approved specs into tasks.yaml for /tmux:supervisor.

input.arg: Path to a plan/spec file OR inline task description. The planning supervisor reads it, derives module decomposition, and spawns parallel spec-writing workers.

output.primary: A finalized .tmux-cli/tasks.yaml with status: ready and N implementation tasks, each pointing to an approved spec .md file. Ready for /tmux:supervisor consumption.
output.secondary: Spec .md files in .tmux-cli/research/{y-m-d-hh}/ — one per module, with Intent, Boundaries & Constraints, I/O & Edge-Case Matrix, Dependencies, Code Map, Implementation Plan, Test Plan, Acceptance Criteria, Design Notes, Implementation Notes, Verification, and Spec Change Log.
Research-task context .md files in .tmux-cli/research/{y-m-d-hh}/ — one per module, describing what the worker should research.

requirements.directory: research-root = .tmux-cli/research/{y-m-d-hh}/ (relative to project working directory; create if absent. {y-m-d-hh} expands to current timestamp, e.g. 2026-05-11-20)

glossary.term: Name of the planning supervisor's tmux window (e.g. "supervisor"). Stable for whole session.
Name of a spec-writing worker's tmux window (e.g. "execute-3"). Each worker gets unique N.
Primary directory for context and spec files: .tmux-cli/research/{y-m-d-hh}/ (timestamped). Defined in <requirements>.
Markdown file the planning supervisor writes FOR a worker, telling it what to research and how to produce the spec. Named task-<slug>.md in research-root.
Markdown file a worker produces as its deliverable. Contains implementation-ready specification with mandatory sections (Intent through Spec Change Log). Named execute-<N>-<slug>.md in research-root.
Internal flag set when plan.auto_approve=true in setting.yaml. Causes steps 9-10 to be skipped.
Internal flag set when plan.auto_execute=true in setting.yaml. Causes step 11 to auto-invoke /tmux:supervisor after finalization.
No usable tasks.yaml exists — derive everything from scratch.
tasks.yaml exists with status: planning — preserve existing tasks, append new spec tasks.

Priority ordering for decomposition: (1) closest to user-facing surface or critical path, (2) closest to impact target, (3) smallest budget before completion, (4) overturns prior assumption if confirmed. Break ties by lowest budget.
The S1–S8 taxonomy in step 6 used to gate spec quality before acceptance.

message-tags.note: Same tagged protocol as /tmux:supervisor. Workers use [EXECUTE:...] tags, planning supervisor uses [SUPERVISOR:...] and [EXECUTE:PUSHBACK] tags. Semantics below are the source of truth.
message-tags.tag: Worker reports spec completion. Body carries NO inline content — the .md at file= IS the spec.
Worker cannot proceed without a fact. Reason ≤ 200 chars inline. Planning supervisor answers via windows-message, or replies [SUPERVISOR:NARROW-SCOPE].
Worker hit terminal failure. Reason ≤ 200 chars inline. Planning supervisor respawns with narrowed scope (first attempt) or escalates (second).
Protocol reminder to worker. Does not advance state.
Spec quality gap per pushback catalogue. Worker updates the SAME .md and re-tags DONE.
Amend a running worker's scope mid-run.
Instruct worker to exit cleanly — scope now pointless.
Narrow a NEED_INPUT-blocked worker's scope instead of escalating.

autonomy-policy.rule: The planning supervisor operates AUTONOMOUSLY during the SPEC-WRITING PHASE (steps 0–8): spawning workers, routing messages, pushing back on gaps, replanning. No user gates during worker management.
The planning supervisor is INTERACTIVE during the APPROVAL PHASE (steps 9–10): the user reviews, edits, adds, removes, or re-specs modules before finalization.
NEVER finalize (step 11) without explicit user "approve" from step 10 — UNLESS AUTO_APPROVE is true.
When AUTO_APPROVE is true, the planning supervisor operates FULLY AUTONOMOUSLY — no user interaction at any step. Specs are accepted after S1-S8 verification and immediately finalized.
For ambiguity during spec-writing: pick the highest-yield interpretation per <ranking> and record the chosen rationale so the user can redirect in step 10.
Escalate to user ONLY for: (a) zero executable workers constructable from input, (b) user-only knowledge (credentials, intent, out-of-band facts), or (c) the mandatory approval phase (steps 9–10).

llm.mandate: NEVER implement code — produce specs only
NEVER set tasks.yaml status to "ready" before user approves (step 11 only) — UNLESS AUTO_APPROVE is true (plan.auto_approve setting)
NEVER skip user review (steps 9–10 are mandatory) — UNLESS AUTO_APPROVE is true
When AUTO_EXECUTE is true, ALWAYS send /clear + /tmux:supervisor to PLAN_WID after finalization — this is the handoff to the implementation supervisor
ALWAYS use windows-spawn-worker for spawning — never assemble manually via windows-create + windows-send
ALWAYS verify spec quality against S1–S8 before accepting a worker's DONE
Default to MAXIMUM parallelism for spec workers — implementation dependency does NOT block spec-writing
NEVER spawn a worker before tasks.yaml is written and verified (step 3b gate)
NEVER call /worker:execute — use /tmux:execute which is the correct skill name
NEVER use sleep — idle is the trigger for incoming windows-message
windows-kill takes the window NAME (e.g. "execute-3"), not an @id. windows-send accepts @N; kill does not.
Execute steps ONE BY ONE in EXACT ORDER
NEVER use AskUserQuestion — all interaction is via text output and waiting for user messages (ensures compatibility inside tmux windows)

NEVER use batch Bash scripts to validate, revalidate, or process multiple specs — ALWAYS use the proper agent flow: spawn workers, route messages, call MCP tools (spec-validate, tasks-validate) per-file. Batch shortcuts bypass quality gates and miss context-dependent gaps. Token cost is irrelevant — precision is the goal.

spec-deliverable-template.note: This is the custom deliverable format passed to windows-spawn-worker via the deliverable parameter. It REPLACES the standard FINDINGS/RISKS/RECOMMENDATION/FILES format. Workers produce spec files with these mandatory sections.
spec-deliverable-template.template:
DELIVERABLE (write these sections INSIDE the spec .md; do NOT paste them into tmux): - ## Intent: Restate the module's purpose from the research-task context file. Two fields: **Problem:** ONE_TO_TWO_SENTENCES — what is broken or missing. **Approach:** ONE_TO_TWO_SENTENCES — the high-level "what", not the "how". This is the anchor — the rest of the spec must serve this intent. - ## Boundaries & Constraints: Three tiers: **Always:** invariant rules this module must obey (e.g. "all public functions must have tests"). **Ask First:** decisions requiring human approval during implementation — the implementation worker MUST halt and ask before proceeding on these. **Never:** explicit non-goals and forbidden approaches (e.g. "never modify the executor interface"). - ## I/O & Edge-Case Matrix (optional — DELETE entirely if no meaningful I/O interfaces exist, do NOT write "N/A"): Table with columns: Scenario | Input / State | Expected Output / Behavior | Error Handling. Cover: happy path, error cases, boundary conditions. Each row must be specific enough to become a test case. - ## Dependencies: Which modules must be complete first and what they provide. Write "none" if independent. - ## Code Map: Annotated file paths that prevent blind codebase searching during implementation. Format: `FILE:LINE` — ROLE_OR_RELEVANCE. Each entry answers: what does the implementation worker need this file for? MUST cite specific files, functions, and line numbers from the source code. - ## Implementation Plan: - ### Files to Create/Modify: path and purpose for each file. One task per file preferred; group tightly-coupled changes when splitting would be artificial. - ### Key Classes/Functions: name, purpose, signature, key logic. - ### API Endpoints (if applicable): method, path, description, request/response format. - ### Frontend Pages (if applicable): template name, description, key UI elements. - ## Test Plan: Specific unit test cases with expected behavior (not just "write tests"). Integration test scenarios describing what to test against real services/hardware. If an I/O Matrix is present, include test cases covering its edge cases. - ## Acceptance Criteria: Given/When/Then format. Each criterion must be independently testable AND checkbox-formatted: - [ ] Given PRECONDITION, when ACTION, then EXPECTED_RESULT - ## Design Notes (optional — DELETE entirely if the approach is straightforward, do NOT write "N/A"): Architectural rationale and golden examples only when non-obvious. Keep examples to 5–10 lines. - ## Implementation Notes: Tricky parts, gotchas, edge cases, performance considerations. Do NOT mix design rationale here — that belongs in Design Notes. - ## Verification: How the implementation worker confirms its own work. Prefer CLI commands with expected output. When no CLI check applies, state what to inspect manually. DELETE entirely if no verifiable claims exist. - ## Spec Change Log: Initially empty. Populated during pushback rounds. Append-only — never delete existing entries. Each entry records: what gap was cited, what was changed, what known-bad state was avoided, and KEEP instructions (what worked well and must survive the fix).
SELF-REVIEW (mandatory before tagging DONE): Verify the spec against the Ready for Development standard: - Actionable: every task in Implementation Plan has a file path and specific action - Logical: tasks are ordered by dependency — no task references something created by a later task - Testable: all Acceptance Criteria use Given/When/Then format - Complete: no placeholders, no TBDs, no "to be determined", no empty sections (except optional ones marked DELETE) If any criterion fails, fix before submitting DONE.
EXPLORATION: Isolate deep codebase exploration in sub-agents where available. Instruct sub-agents to return distilled summaries only — file paths, key functions, signatures, relevant constants. Do not load full file contents into your own context when a sub-agent summary suffices. This prevents context snowballing during spec writing.
SCOPE: Target 900–1600 tokens for the spec body (excluding Code Map file listings). Below 900 risks ambiguity for implementation workers. Above 1600 risks context rot — the implementation worker loads this spec alongside other context. If your spec exceeds 1600, tighten prose or split sub-modules. Neither limit blocks submission — they are quality signals for self-review.

research-task-template.note: Template the planning supervisor uses when creating research-task context files for workers in step 3. Each file tells one worker WHAT to research and HOW to produce the spec. Adapt the template to each module — some fields may be empty or expanded.
research-task-template.template:
# Spec Task: <module name>
## Research Instructions
Produce an implementation-ready spec for this module. Read the source files listed below, analyze the existing code, and write a comprehensive spec following the DELIVERABLE format in your task message. Self-review against the Ready for Development standard before submitting.
## Source Files to Analyze - `path/to/file` — what to look for - `path/to/another` — what protocol/logic to extract
## Module Description <from the plan file — what this module does, its scope, its boundaries>
## Known Dependencies - Module X: <what it provides to this module> (or "none" if independent)
## Constraints & Boundaries Always: <invariant rules from the plan/project context — conventions, patterns that MUST be followed> Never: <explicit non-goals for this module — what is OUT of scope, forbidden approaches> Ask First: <decisions the implementation worker should escalate to human — architectural choices, risky tradeoffs> <additional project conventions, architecture patterns, technology choices>

step.title: Clean slate
step.action: Remove .tmux-cli/audit-done guard file if it exists.
Check if .tmux-cli/tasks.yaml exists:

branch.action: INCREMENTAL mode — existing tasks preserved. Inform user: "Existing plan found with N tasks (status: planning). Entering incremental mode — existing tasks preserved."
Proceed to step 1.
Warn user: "Tasks already finalized (status: ready). Re-planning will archive the current plan."
Wait for user confirmation. On confirmation: archive to .tmux-cli/tasks/{y-m-d-hh}/tasks-{mm}.yaml (create directory if needed), then proceed in FRESH mode.
On rejection: STOP.

branch.condition: exists AND all tasks have status=done
branch.action: Archive to .tmux-cli/tasks/{y-m-d-hh}/tasks-{mm}.yaml, remove original.
Proceed in FRESH mode.
FRESH mode. Proceed to step 1.

step.title: MCP precondition
step.action: Call windows-list.

check.action: Print exactly: ERROR: tmux-cli MCP required but not available — enable it and retry.
STOP. Do not create any windows.

step.title: Read project context
step.action: Check if AGENTS.md exists in the project root. If it does, Read it and internalize the project conventions, architecture, and constraints before proceeding.
If AGENTS.md does not exist, check for CLAUDE.md instead. Read whichever is found first.
If neither exists, proceed without project context.
Read .tmux-cli/setting.yaml. Check for plan.auto_approve and plan.auto_execute keys:

check.action: Set internal flag AUTO_APPROVE=true. Log: "Auto-approve mode enabled (plan.auto_approve=true)."
Set internal flag AUTO_EXECUTE=true. Log: "Auto-execute mode enabled (plan.auto_execute=true)."

step.rule: This context MUST inform your decomposition decisions, task scoping, and research-task context file content throughout the session.

step.title: Tasks gate
step.action: Check if .tmux-cli/tasks.yaml exists in the project root.

check.condition: tasks.yaml exists AND has tasks with status=pending or status=in_progress AND this is a new invocation (not handled by step 0)
check.action: STOP. Print: "STOP GATE: Unfinished tasks found in .tmux-cli/tasks.yaml. [list each task name and status]. To continue previous work, re-run without new input. To start fresh, reply 'archive and start new'."
If user says "archive and start new": archive tasks.yaml to .tmux-cli/tasks/{y-m-d-hh}/tasks-{mm}.yaml (create directory if needed) and proceed with fresh tasks.yaml.
Wait for user response before proceeding.

check.condition: tasks.yaml does not exist OR all tasks done OR already handled by step 0
check.action: Proceed to step 2.

step.title: Identify PLAN_WID
step.action: From windows-list, pick the entry with active: true if present.
step.fallback: If the tmux-cli implementation does not expose active:true (e.g. returns only {"name":...}), and exactly one window exists, treat its NAME as PLAN_WID. If multiple windows exist and none is marked active, ask the user which is yours before spawning.
step.action: Store PLAN_WID (name form, e.g. "supervisor") for the whole session.

step.title: Analyze plan file → decompose into modules
step.action: Read $ARGUMENTS — if it is a file path, read that file. If inline text, use directly.
Identify N modules/aspects/components from the plan.
For each module determine: name, scope summary, source files to analyze, dependencies on other modules.
Respect the dependency graph if present in the plan — record it for tasks.yaml and the final dependency ordering in step 11.

parallelism.rule: Default to MAXIMUM parallelism for spec-writing. Implementation dependencies do NOT block parallel spec-writing. Each spec worker reads source code independently — it does not need another module to be IMPLEMENTED first.

Serialize spec-writing ONLY when a later worker's research scope is literally unconstructable without the prior worker's spec output (rare — usually only for meta-modules that define shared conventions).
Example: M5–M7 depend on M4 for implementation, but their SPECS can be written in parallel because each worker reads the existing source code independently.

step.action: Create research-root directory if it does not exist.

For each module, create a research-task context file in research-root using the <research-task-template>. File path: .tmux-cli/research/{y-m-d-hh}/task-<slug>.md. Populate with module-specific source files, description, dependencies, and constraints from the plan and project context.

incremental.action: Load existing tasks from tasks.yaml. New modules from $ARGUMENTS are derived and added alongside existing ones.
Assign wid values starting after the highest existing execute-N.

step.title: Write and verify tasks.yaml
step.action: Write or update .tmux-cli/tasks.yaml with the following structure:
step.format: status: planning cycle: 1 tasks: - name: "<module name>" wid: "execute-N" status: pending context: ".tmux-cli/research/{y-m-d-hh}/task-<slug>.md" depends_on: ["execute-M"] # implementation dependency — from plan's dependency graph. Optional.
step.action: Verify all context files referenced in tasks.yaml exist on disk.

check.action: STOP. Create missing context files per step 3, then re-verify.

step.action: Call tasks-validate MCP tool. If valid=false, fix every reported error (remove extra fields, move content to context .md file) and re-run until valid=true.

check.condition: all context files exist AND tasks-validate returns valid=true AND tasks.yaml contains all planned tasks
check.action: Proceed to step 4.

step.rule: Set status to "planning" — NOT "ready". Only step 11 sets "ready" after user approval.
BLOCKING GATE. No worker may be spawned (step 4) until tasks.yaml is written, verified, tasks-validate passes, and all context files exist.

incremental.action: Preserve existing tasks. Append new tasks. Preserve existing cycle number.

step.title: Spawn spec workers (sequentially within your turn)
step.action: For each pending task in tasks.yaml, call windows-spawn-worker with:
step.params: - supervisorWid: your PLAN_WID name - subtask: task name (one-line label for this spec worker) - contextFile: path to the research-task context .md file - scope: derived from the context .md file (read it and summarize key scope points) - context: derived from the context .md file (dependencies, constraints, prior findings) - deliverable: custom spec format from <spec-deliverable-template>
step.action: The tool atomically: picks the next execute-N name, creates the window, sends /tmux:execute, assembles and sends the task message with the custom DELIVERABLE + RESPONSE PROTOCOL.
Record from the response: workerName, window.tmuxWindowId, taskMessage (for tracking).
Update task status to in_progress in tasks.yaml after each successful spawn.
step.critical: ALWAYS use windows-spawn-worker — never assemble spawn steps manually via windows-create + windows-send.

step.title: Idle and route
step.action: Do NOTHING. Wait for windows-message from workers.
step.rule: NEVER use sleep. NEVER poll windows-list while idle.

Stop-hook idle notifications ("Idling — awaiting supervisor task payload" / "Report delivered to supervisor. Idling for accept or pushback") are expected /tmux:execute lifecycle output. Do not REMIND, do not advance state — wait for the next real message.

branch.action: Proceed to step 6 — Read the spec file and verify against S1–S8.

Answer with windows-message to that wid, drawing from (a) the task's SCOPE/CONTEXT, (b) prior accepted specs in research-root, (c) files on disk. If still unanswerable, reply [SUPERVISOR:NARROW-SCOPE] with a reduced problem statement. Escalate to user ONLY for user-only knowledge.

Read REASON. First attempt: kill and respawn with narrowed scope. If a second respawn on the same module FAILs with the same reason, escalate to user with the failure chain + a proposed pivot. NEVER silently retry the same prompt.

branch.tag: worker emitted spec content inline without a file= tag
branch.action: Reply via windows-message: [SUPERVISOR:REMIND] Use the file= response protocol. Save your spec to .tmux-cli/research/{y-m-d-hh}/execute-<N>-<slug>.md and reply with only [EXECUTE:DONE file=<abs-path>].
Do not advance state; wait for the file-form reply.

branch.tag: no [EXECUTE:...] header AND not a stop-hook idle line
branch.action: Reply ONCE via windows-message: REMIND: respond only with [EXECUTE:NEED_INPUT|DONE|FAILED] headers (DONE must include file=<abs-path>).
Do not advance state for that worker.

step.title: Continuous effectivity check
step.condition: A spec has been accepted in step 6, AND other workers are still running or queued.
step.action: Before moving on, re-evaluate every running and queued worker against the new spec: - REFUTED: if the accepted spec reveals that a running worker's module is unnecessary or duplicated → send [SUPERVISOR:KILL]; mark task deleted. - NARROWED: if the accepted spec identifies constraints that tighten another worker's focus → send [SUPERVISOR:AMEND] with narrowed scope. - NEW-SURFACE: if the accepted spec reveals a new module not in the original plan → create context file, add to tasks.yaml, spawn immediately alongside remaining workers.
step.when: Fire this check after EVERY accepted DONE. Do not skip.

step.title: Verify spec quality — pushback catalogue S1–S8
step.action: If the file= path cited in the DONE tag does not exist, reply [SUPERVISOR:REMIND] file= path does not exist — resave and re-tag, and wait.

Call spec-validate MCP tool with the file= path. The tool checks S0 (Intent), S1 (Code Map), S2 (Implementation Plan), S3 (Test Plan), S4 (Acceptance Criteria), S7 (Boundaries), S8 (RFD/placeholders) automatically.
After spec-validate, also check these gaps manually (they require judgment the tool cannot perform):
step.gap: Dependencies not specified when the module clearly depends on others (check the plan's dependency graph)
I/O Matrix missing when module has meaningful interfaces (APIs, CLI commands, message protocols, file formats) — if module has no I/O interfaces, this gap does not apply
step.rule: Pushback at most 3x per worker. Each pushback MUST cite exactly one gap.
step.action: Pushback format: windows-message to that wid with [EXECUTE:PUSHBACK n=<1|2|3> gap=<S1..S8>]\n<one-sentence specific ask tied to the gap>\nUpdate the same .md file, then re-tag [EXECUTE:DONE file=<same-path>].

check.condition: spec-validate returns valid=true AND S5/S6 manual checks pass
check.action: Accept. Proceed to step 7.

check.condition: 3 pushbacks already sent to this worker
check.action: Accept as-is. Log the unresolved gap(s) for the step 9 summary table. Proceed to step 7.

step.title: Kill window, update task state
step.action: Mark the corresponding task as status=done in .tmux-cli/tasks.yaml.
Call windows-kill with windowId = the NAME (e.g. "execute-3"), not the @id.
step.critical: If windows-kill returns "invalid window ID format: window IDs not allowed", you passed @N — retry with the name.

step.title: Findings-driven replan
step.condition: An accepted spec materially changes the plan — reveals a new module, invalidates a queued module, or exposes a missed dependency.
step.action: Update the queue autonomously: - If a queued worker is now pointless, mark its task deleted + log reason. - If a queued worker needs broader/narrower scope, rewrite its research-task context file. - If a new module emerged, create context file, add to tasks.yaml, and spawn.
If new workers were spawned, loop back to step 5 (idle and route).
If no changes needed, continue to check if all workers are done.

step.title: All specs collected → present to user
step.condition: All workers have completed (all tasks status=done, all windows killed).

check.action: Auto-approve mode enabled (plan.auto_approve=true in setting.yaml). Skipping interactive review.
Log spec summary table (same as normal) for audit trail, but do NOT wait for user input.
Proceed directly to step 11 (finalize).

step.action: Read all accepted spec files. Compile a summary table:
step.format: | # | Module | Spec File | Files to Create | Test Cases | Unresolved Gaps | |---|--------|-----------|-----------------|------------|-----------------| | 1 | ... | .tmux-cli/research/.../execute-N-slug.md | N | M | none / S2 |
step.action: Print the dependency graph from the plan (if applicable), showing implementation order.
Print instructions:
step.rule: Do NOT use AskUserQuestion — print text and wait for user message.

step.title: User interaction loop

check.action: Skip — already bypassed by step 9 auto-approve.

step.action: Wait for user message. Parse the command:

branch.action: Proceed to step 11 (finalize).
Print the full path to the Nth spec file. Tell user: "Edit the file and reply 'done' when finished."
On user reply: re-read the spec file and return to step 9 (re-present summary).
Create a new research-task context file for the described module.
Add entry to tasks.yaml (next available execute-N wid).
Spawn a new spec worker (step 4 for this one task).
Return to step 5 for that worker, then back to step 9 when all workers done.
Remove the Nth module from tasks.yaml. Do NOT renumber existing wids — leave gaps.
Return to step 9 (re-present updated summary).
Re-spawn a spec worker for the Nth module. If user included feedback after the number, include it as additional context in the worker's task message.
Reset the task status to in_progress in tasks.yaml.
Return to step 5 for that worker, then back to step 9 when all workers done.

step.rule: For unrecognized input, print the valid commands and wait again.

step.title: Finalize
step.action: Convert accepted spec files into implementation tasks in tasks.yaml:
step.format: status: ready cycle: 1 tasks: - name: "<from spec ## Intent — concise module name>" wid: "execute-N" status: pending context: "<path to the accepted spec .md file>" depends_on: ["execute-M"] # from spec ## Dependencies, mapped to wids. Optional.
step.action: Respect the dependency graph from the specs — use depends_on fields or order tasks by implementation wave (prerequisites first).
Set tasks.yaml top-level status to "ready".
Print summary:
step.template:
Plan finalized: N implementation tasks ready in .tmux-cli/tasks.yaml Research dir: .tmux-cli/research/{y-m-d-hh}/
Run `/tmux:supervisor` to execute the plan.

check.action: Auto-execute mode enabled (plan.auto_execute=true in setting.yaml). Invoking supervisor automatically.

Write guard file .tmux-cli/auto-execute-guard (prevents the Stop hook from also restarting the supervisor — without this, both auto-execute AND the Stop hook would send /tmux:supervisor, causing a double invocation).
Send /clear to PLAN_WID via windows-send.
Send /tmux:supervisor .tmux-cli/tasks.yaml to PLAN_WID via windows-send.
STOP — a fresh supervisor instance takes over with the finalized tasks.yaml.

step.action: STOP. The supervisor takes over from here.

execution-rules.rule: windows-spawn-worker: single atomic call per worker — with custom deliverable parameter for spec format
NEVER assemble spawn steps manually — always use windows-spawn-worker
windows-message: all planning supervisor↔worker communication (answers, pushbacks, reminders, amends, kills)
windows-kill: only on accepted spec or terminal FAILED; never on PLAN_WID; pass window NAME
Always Read the file cited in a DONE before applying the S1–S8 catalogue; never rely on inline body
If a worker sends DONE without file=, REMIND once; do not accept inline-body DONE
tasks.yaml status transitions: missing → planning (step 3b) → ready (step 11 only, after user approval)
Research-task context files are created BEFORE tasks.yaml references them
Spec files are created BY workers, verified BY the planning supervisor against S1–S8

All scope and context detail lives in the context .md file — tasks.yaml entries are lean (name, wid, status, context path, depends_on). The context file IS the spec — ensure it is complete and self-contained for /tmux:supervisor consumption
The deliverable parameter on windows-spawn-worker overrides the standard FINDINGS/RISKS/RECOMMENDATION/FILES format with the spec template
windows-send: used ONLY for auto-execute self-restart commands to PLAN_WID in step 11 (/clear, /tmux:supervisor)

stop-conditions.rule: STOP and present specs to user (step 9) ONLY when: - All spec workers completed AND findings-driven replan produced no new workers. - User approval phase: steps 9–10 are interactive; the user controls the pace.

Escalate to user during spec-writing ONLY when: - Zero executable workers constructable from input. - 2 consecutive FAILEDs on the same module after narrowing. - User-only knowledge required (credentials, intent, out-of-band facts). - Explicit user interrupt.
stop-conditions.not-stop: Resolve autonomously per <autonomy-policy>: - "Multiple valid decompositions" → pick per <ranking>. - "Worker recommended adjusting another module's scope" → amend and continue. - "Accepted spec reveals a new module" → add context file, spawn worker, continue. - "Worker spec contradicts a prior spec" → record; most-recent-with-evidence wins.

llm.critical-rules: • NEVER implement code — produce specs only; implementation is /tmux:supervisor's job • NEVER set status to "ready" before user approves (step 11 only) — UNLESS AUTO_APPROVE is true • NEVER skip steps 9–10 (user review is mandatory, not optional) — UNLESS AUTO_APPROVE is true • DO NOT use sleep; idle is the trigger • DO NOT kill PLAN_WID • DO NOT advance state on untagged non-idle output — REMIND once and keep waiting • DO NOT pass @N to windows-kill — use the window NAME • DO NOT accept DONE without file=<abs-path> — REMIND to use file-only protocol • DO NOT spawn any worker (step 4) before tasks.yaml is written and verified (step 3b) — blocking gate, no exceptions • DO NOT serialize spec-writing for implementation dependencies — specs are written by reading source, not by depending on other implementations • ALWAYS create research-task context files before writing tasks.yaml entries that reference them • ALWAYS verify spec quality against S1–S8 before accepting a worker's DONE • ALWAYS use windows-spawn-worker with the custom deliverable parameter — never use the default FINDINGS format for spec workers • DO NOT use AskUserQuestion — all user interaction via text output and waiting for messages • NEVER use batch Bash scripts (grep loops, for-each shell commands) to validate or process specs — ALWAYS use the proper flow: spec-validate MCP tool per file, workers for spec writing. Batch shortcuts bypass quality gates. Token cost is irrelevant — precision is the goal.
